title: Heidegger
essence: Vervaeke examines Heidegger's philosophical project as a prophetic response to the meaning crisis. The central thread is Heidegger's critique of the history of metaphysics as the history of nihilism — the systematic forgetfulness of being that results from becoming trapped in the having mode. From Heidegger's essay on the essence of truth, Vervaeke draws the key insight that truth as correctness (correspondence between statements and reality) is grounded in a deeper attunement — a comportment toward things that makes the agent-arena relationship possible in the first place. This attunement is identified with relevance realization, and its forgetfulness is identified as modal confusion. The essay on Angelus Silesius's rose poem illustrates the alternative: physis, the self-grounding blossoming of things that simultaneously shine into our experience and withdraw beyond our framing — the thing beyond itself. Truth as aletheia — unconcealment, a deep remembering that is simultaneously a disclosure of things shining and withdrawing — is the response to forgetfulness. Dreyfus is identified as the interpreter who connects Heidegger's thought to 4E cognitive science through the notion of optimal grip. Corbin's reading of Heidegger as anticipating gnosis — the transformative participatory knowing in which self-knowledge and world-knowledge are coupled — is introduced. The problem of onto-theology — understanding being in terms of a supreme being — is identified as the metaphysical error that deepens nihilism.
keyClaims: The history of metaphysics is the history of nihilism: a systematic forgetfulness of being produced by modal confusion — getting trapped in the having mode and forgetting the being mode.
Truth as correspondence between statements and reality is grounded in a deeper attunement that Heidegger calls the essence of truth — aletheia, an unconcealment that must be actively remembered.
The thing beyond itself — simultaneously shining into experience and withdrawing from our framing — is the ontological structure that grounds both relevance realization and sacredness.
Onto-theology — understanding being in terms of a supreme being — is the modal confusion that deepens nihilism by trying to capture the inexhaustible in a propositional object.
Corbin reads Heidegger as anticipating gnosis: the transformative participatory knowing where self-knowledge and world-knowledge are coupled in a dynamical conformity.

concepts.summary: Heidegger's project is read as the attempt to recover the being mode from the deep forgetfulness of modal confusion into which the history of metaphysics has driven Western thought.
The history of metaphysics is identified as the deepening of modal confusion — trying to grasp being within the having mode — leading to nihilism.
Heidegger's attunement is interpreted as transjective: not subjective experience but the underlying relation that makes subject-object experience possible.
Heidegger's attunement and Dreyfus's optimal grip are aligned with relevance realization as the non-propositional machinery that grounds the agent-arena relationship.
Corbin reads Heidegger's participatory knowing as gnosis — salvic, redemptive participatory knowing in which self and world are transformed together.
Heidegger's attunement as the ground of the agent-arena relationship is connected to worldview attunement: the way agent and arena are shaped to each other below the propositional level.
Dreyfus explicitly bridges Heidegger and 4E cognitive science; his Being-in-the-World and What Computers Can't Do are named as founding texts of this connection.
Dreyfus's notion of optimal grip is identified as the cognitive-scientific articulation of Heidegger's attunement and relevance realization.
Heidegger is examined as the central prophet in depth; the connections to Dreyfus, Corbin, and speculative realism (Harman) are elaborated.

people.summary: The primary subject: his critique of metaphysics as nihilism, his notion of aletheia, Dasein, attunement, onto-theology, and physis are all examined in depth.

His interpretation of Heidegger and his founding role in 4E cognitive science (optimal grip, What Computers Can't Do, Being-in-the-World) are examined as the bridge between Heidegger and cognitive science.
His reading of Heidegger as anticipating gnosis is introduced; Avens's book The New Gnosis is cited as the source for the Corbin-Heidegger connection.
Heidegger's commentary on Angelus Silesius's rose poem — itself an interpretation of Eckhart — connects Heidegger to the Rhineland mystical tradition.
Credited with developing the embodiment aspect of Heidegger's project; Vervaeke argues his work partially addresses the participatory knowing gap Heidegger identifies in Husserl.
Philia sophia vs. philia nikia — the love of wisdom vs. the love of victory — is invoked as the Platonic framing for Heidegger's critique of propositional metaphysics.
Dreyfus and Taylor's Retrieving Realism is cited as an example of using Heidegger and 4E cognitive science to recover contact epistemology.

crossRefs.context: Heidegger was positioned as the central prophet and Dasein was introduced; this episode provides the full examination.
Corbin's development of Heidegger's gnosis through the imaginal and the divine double is the subject of the next episode.

quotableMoments.quote: Facts and rules are, by themselves, meaningless. To capture what Heidegger calls significance or involvement, they must be assigned relevance.
quotableMoments.context: Dreyfus's statement of the frame problem, quoted to establish the Heidegger-cognitive science connection via relevance.
